doc.title: Lessor Operating Lease
doc.event: Boone Properties leases a floor of its building (building cost $900,000, 30-year life, no residual value) to a tenant under a 5-year operating lease for $60,000 per year, received at each year-end. Prepare Boone's entries for the first year.

lines.why: Straight-line revenue — operating lessor recognizes rent as earned.

entries.label: Depreciate the building — it never left Boone's books

lines.why: 900,000 ÷ 30 years. The lessor keeps the asset AND keeps depreciating it.
lines.trap: No receivable, no derecognition — that's the whole point of an operating lease for the lessor.

memorize.body: Operating lessor keeps the asset on its books and depreciates it. No Lease Receivable, no Sales Revenue, no derecognition.

questions.prompt: Rent $60,000, depreciation $30,000. Boone's net income effect from the lease in year 1 = ?

distractors.feedback: You counted the cash but skipped the depreciation. The building stayed on Boone's books, so its 30,000 depreciation offsets the rent.

build.scaffold: Two everyday entries: rent receipt and depreciation. If you're derecognizing the building, you've classified the lease wrong.
